ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for honey lemon drink. FOODON_03301710 (lemonade) is the closest parent category covering lemon-based sweetened beverages.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (Lemon juice drink) is the closest defensible parent. No specific honey lemon drink code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for honey lemon drink as a specific food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 ml (300 g), equivalent to a typical takeaway cup of cold honey lemon drink as served in Singapore casual dining and hot pot restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Honey lemon drink — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Honey (FDC ID 169640) and Lemon juice raw (FDC ID 167747), combined in typical proportions (approximately 15 g honey per 100 ml diluted drink with lemon juice and water)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for honey lemon drink exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for honey (FDC 169640) and raw lemon juice (FDC 167747). Typical Singapore preparation uses approximately 15 g honey diluted in 85 ml water with 10–15 ml fresh lemon juice per 100 ml serving. Nutrient values are proportionally blended: honey contributes ~47 kcal, ~12.7 g carbohydrate, ~12.4 g sugar per 15 g; lemon juice contributes ~2 kcal, ~0.7 g carbohydrate, ~0.2 g sugar per 15 ml; water contributes zero. Final per-100 g values reflect this blend.

gi_source.title: Honey, pure — GI value 58 (glucose reference); analogous estimate applied to honey lemon drink based on honey as the primary glycemic contributor
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for honey lemon drink. GI estimated from pure honey (GI 58, medium, University of Sydney GI Database) as honey is the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source. Lemon juice contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (58 × 33.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 19.1, rounded to 19.2. Available carbohydrate per serving = 11.0 g per 100 g × 3 = 33.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~2 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Honey lemon drink is very low in sodium (~6 mg per 300 ml serving) and poses minimal direct risk for hypertension; however, the moderate sugar content should be considered in the context of overall dietary pattern and weight management, as excess sugar intake can indirectly elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 58) and significant free sugar content (~31.8 g per 300 ml serving) from honey
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume honey lemon drink with caution; a 300 ml serving delivers approximately 31.8 g of free sugars and a glycemic load of ~19, which can cause a meaningful postprandial blood glucose rise and should be counted within the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and saturated fat; moderate free sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Honey lemon drink contains essentially no fat or saturated fat, posing minimal direct cardiovascular lipid risk; however, regular consumption of high-sugar beverages is associated with elevated triglycerides and should be limited in individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Honey Lemon Drink (Cold) — known in Chinese as 蜜糖柠檬 (冷) — is a popular non-alcoholic chilled beverage widely served in Singapore's Chinese casual dining restaurants, hot pot establishments, and zi char stalls. It is prepared by dissolving pure honey in water and combining it with freshly squeezed or concentrated lemon juice, then serving over ice or pre-chilled. The drink has a bright, tangy-sweet flavour profile: the natural floral sweetness of honey is balanced by the sharp citrus acidity of lemon, producing a refreshing and palate-cleansing beverage. The texture is thin and watery, similar to diluted lemonade. Per 100 g, the drink provides approximately 42 kcal, 11 g of carbohydrate (predominantly free sugars from honey at ~10.6 g), negligible protein (~0.1 g), and essentially no fat. A standard 300 ml serving delivers roughly 126 kcal and 33 g of carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on honey as the primary glycemic contributor. Clinically, the drink is low in sodium and fat, making it broadly acceptable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management in moderate quantities; however, its significant free sugar content warrants caution for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing weight. Vitamin C content (~5.5 mg per 100 g) provides a modest antioxidant contribution from the lemon juice component. The drink is vegetarian and halal-friendly, and is gluten-free and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for honey lemon drink exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary approved regional database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for honey and lemon juice in typical Singapore preparation proportions. GI is estimated from pure honey (University of Sydney GI Database, GI 58). Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach; values are internally consistent and defensible but not derived from a direct food composition measurement of this specific product.
